summary:
Peasant revolts across 14th-century Europe — including the French Jacquerie (1358), the English Peasants' Revolt of 1381 ([[event:3017|Wat Tyler's rebellion]]), and numerous other uprisings — represented an unprecedented wave of social upheaval driven by the intersection of the [[event:95|Black Death's]] demographic disruption, the labor shortage that followed it, and the attempts of landlords and governments to maintain feudal obligations and suppress wages in defiance of the new bargaining power that labor scarcity had granted to surviving peasants. The dramatic labor shortage following the plague's 30–50% population mortality meant that surviving peasants and workers could demand — and obtain — dramatically higher wages; when landlords and governments responded with legal caps on wages and enforcement of traditional feudal services, violent resistance followed.

The Jacquerie (1358) erupted in the Île-de-France following French military disasters in the [[event:2235|Hundred Years' War]] and heavy taxation; peasants attacked noble estates with unexpected ferocity before being suppressed with mass executions. The English Peasants' Revolt (1381) was more organized and reached further — rebels marched on London, occupied the Tower, and killed the Archbishop of Canterbury; the teenage King Richard II made promises at Mile End that he subsequently broke. The Flemish peasant revolts, German peasant wars, and other episodes constituted what some historians have called a 'general crisis' of European feudalism — not successful revolutions, but challenges that accelerated the gradual dismantling of serfdom in Western Europe over the following century and demonstrated that the medieval social order was not eternally stable.

read_more_links.title: Black Death labor shortage peasant power
